---
title: "4 · Physics — Part 1: Hydrology"
---

Groundwater level and soil moisture are two faces of one subsurface water balance — the saturated
store below the water table and the vadose store above it — and both are modelled along the same
spectrum from process physics to pure statistics. We give them equal footing, then enforce a closed
water budget at every pixel.

## How GWL and soil moisture are modelled — three paradigms

**Physical / process models.** The reference is the 3-D groundwater flow equation, solved by MODFLOW
and ParFlow [@maxwell2015connections]:
$$S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (K \nabla h) + R,$$
with head $h$, hydraulic conductivity $K$, specific storage $S_s$, and source/sink $R$. In the vadose
zone the analogue is the Richards equation, routinely reduced for regional work to a
Thornthwaite–Mather soil-water balance [@thornthwaite1957water]. These are physically consistent but
demand aquifer geometry and hydraulic properties poorly known at regional scale; @reinecke2020importance
and @degraaf2015global show that global models at 0.5°–10 km cannot resolve the water-table variability
hazards need. Two process building blocks we reuse below are baseflow recession [@brutsaert1977recession],
which sets a water-table drainage timescale, and the TOPMODEL topographic-wetness closure
[@beven1979physically], which converts a mean deficit into a spatial saturation pattern; recharge — the
vadose-to-saturated flux that couples the two stores — is itself a modelling choice with
method-dependent spread [@scanlon2002choosing].

**Statistical interpolation.** @fan2013global regressed water-table depth on 17 terrain/climate/soil
variables to the first global 1 km product, later improved by the ML-ensemble HydroGEN [@ma2023wtd;
@ma2026highres]. These share four limitations: DEM-tile artifacts, elevation bias (valley floors
indistinguishable from ridge tops at equal elevation), no temporal variability, and severe western-US
extrapolation from sparse wells — HydroGEN still shows a +7.2 m bias / 20.5 m RMSE against held-out PNW
wells. The soil-moisture analogue is digital soil mapping: pedotransfer functions [@saxton2006soil]
applied to ML-mapped soil properties [@nauman2024solus; @ramcharan2018soil], and regression kriging
[@hengl2007regression], which separates a deterministic trend from kriged residuals.

**Data-driven / time-series.** At gauged sites, LSTM networks reach state-of-the-art one-step GWL
prediction [@wunsch2021groundwater; @liesch2019aquifer; @shen2018deeplearning] but do not generalise to
ungauged locations; transfer-function-noise models [@vonasmuth2002tfn; @bakker2019timeseries] and the
Pastas framework [@collenteur2019pastas] give parsimonious per-well impulse responses; and climate-index
regression links GWL to SPI, ENSO, and PDO [@bloomfield2013analysis; @kuss2014multi]. Random forests on
terrain+geology regionalise groundwater properties [@rodriguez2014nitrate; @marcais2017prospecting].

**Hybrid — and the gap.** True hybrids pairing a spatial ML/geostatistical baseline with a temporal
physics component are rare [@konapala2020hybrid; @heudorfer2019groundwater; @cuthbert2019global]. No
published product combines HAND-based regression kriging, β-coefficient response maps, **and** a
pixel-wise water budget delivered with provenance for geotechnical/geomorphological consumers.

## Our ground: the constrained water budget

Rather than solve the flow equation or memorise wells, we **enforce a closed water balance at every
90 m cell** (`src/models/water_budget.py`) and let observations anchor it. Over the fine
SOLUS→Saxton–Rawls storage envelope [@saxton2006soil], each cell steps a balance of: vertical
infiltration and recharge, capillary rise, evapotranspiration, saturated-area runoff, lateral
interflow, and groundwater discharge to the stream network.

The root-zone store $S=\theta\,z_r$ (mm over root depth $z_r$, itself derived from soil thickness
rather than assumed constant) is stepped against the Saxton–Rawls limits $S_x=\theta_x z_r$ ($x\in\{wp,fc,sat\}$), enforcing the closed balance

$$
S_t = S_{t-1} + C_t + I_t - Q_t - E_t - R_t, \qquad \theta_t = S_t/z_r,
$$

with the flux closures (mm month⁻¹; $[x]_+ \equiv \max(x,0)$ and $[x]_0^1 \equiv \min(\max(x,0),\,1)$
clamps to $[0,1]$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t &= \gamma\,[\,S-S_{fc}\,]_+ && \text{recharge (deep percolation)},\\
Q_t &= [\,S+I_t-S_{sat}\,]_+ && \text{saturation-excess runoff},\\
E_t &= \min\!\big(\mathrm{PET}_t,\ [\,S-S_{wp}\,]_+\big) && \text{evapotranspiration},\\
C_t &= C_{\max}\,\Big[\tfrac{(z_r+z_f)-d_{t-1}}{z_r+z_f}\Big]_0^1\,\tfrac{S_{sat}-S}{S_{sat}-S_{wp}} && \text{capillary rise},
\end{aligned}
$$

and the water table advanced as a recharge-fed, laterally-redistributed linear reservoir

$$
h_t = h_{t-1} + \frac{R_t}{S_y} - \frac{h_{t-1}}{\tau},\quad d_t = d_0 - h_t; \qquad
d_i = \bar d - m\,(\mathrm{TWI}_i - \overline{\mathrm{TWI}}),
$$

with specific yield $S_y$, recession time $\tau$ [@brutsaert1977recession], capillary fringe $z_f$, and
the TOPMODEL slope $m$ [@beven1979physically]. This is the *integrated, per-pixel* counterpart of the
3-D flow equation above: we do not solve the PDE, we **enforce its mass balance at every cell** — exact
by construction — and let observations anchor the coefficients. The vertical exchange between soil
moisture and the water table (recharge $\leftrightarrow$ capillary rise) and the downhill routing of
excess water are therefore represented, not assumed away; this is the sense in which the two water
states are *coupled* rather than merely co-located.

## Timestep

The budget integrates at the timestep of its forcing. This is a physical choice, not a convenience: a
storm-scale forecast aggregated to a monthly mean loses the event signal it exists to provide, and a
month of rain applied as a single lump instantly oversaturates the column, generating spurious
saturation-excess runoff while starving recharge.

| 400 mm over one month | runoff | recharge |
|---|---|---|
| applied as one monthly lump | 200 mm (spurious) | 84 mm (starved) |
| daily, uniform | 0 mm | 322 mm |
| daily, 3-day atmospheric river | 54 mm (physical) | 305 mm |

Three of the coefficients are **per-month rates** — baseflow recession, capillary rise, and gravity
drainage — and each is converted to the step.

::: {.callout-warning}
## A lumped monthly drainage fraction has no meaning at daily resolution
A gravity-drainage fraction of $0.6$ per month implies an e-folding timescale of
$30.44/\ln(2.5) \approx 33$ days. Gravity drainage above field capacity is a **1–3 day** process, so
propagating the monthly fraction to a daily step drains the column roughly 15× too slowly. Sub-monthly
runs therefore use a physical drainage timescale rather than a converted monthly fraction. **The two
timesteps are not equivalent, and a calibration performed at one does not transfer to the other.**
:::

## Runoff generation

Runoff **generation** belongs to the budget — it is a sink, and omitting it biases $\theta$ and the
water table high. Runoff **routing** does not: converting the source term into a discharge hydrograph
is a surface-routing problem, handed to LandLab
([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)).

The dominant humid-temperate mechanism is **variable source area**: rain cannot infiltrate where there
is no space between the water table and the surface,

$$Q_t = \left[\;I_t - S_y\, d_{wt}\cdot 1000\;\right]_+ ,$$

which is continuous rather than a switch — at a 1 m water table, 224 mm of a 400 mm input runs off
while 167 mm still recharges — and concentrates on the shallow-water-table cells that are the valley
floors and riparian corridors.

**Infiltration-excess (Hortonian) runoff is not represented.** It is intensity-driven and requires
sub-daily rainfall: 68 mm/day is only 2.8 mm/hr, far below these soils' $K_{sat}$ (15–55 mm/hr). Real
storms burst well above their daily mean, which is an argument for driving the budget at the native
sub-daily step of the forcing rather than aggregating it.

## Lateral interflow

Water draining out of the root zone does not all reach the water table. On a slope it partitions
between a vertical velocity $\sim K_v$ and a downslope one $\sim K_h \tan\beta$; with the anisotropy
$K_a = K_h/K_v$ of layered forest soils, a large fraction leaves **downslope** as subsurface stormflow
and reaches a stream within days:

$$f_{lat} = \frac{K_a \tan\beta}{1 + K_a \tan\beta}.$$

Flat ground ($\tan\beta = 0$) gives $f_{lat}=0$ — valley floors recharge, hillslopes shed. Without
this term the budget converts essentially all precipitation into groundwater recharge.

## Groundwater discharge to the stream network

The water table is bounded by the rivers. Groundwater standing above the local drainage flows to it,
and HAND is by construction the *Height Above Nearest Drainage*, so the head driving that discharge is
$[\mathrm{HAND} - d_{wt}]_+$ and the table relaxes toward the drainage elevation. The sink is
**self-limiting**: the higher the table climbs above the river, the harder it drains, so recharge
cannot accumulate without bound. Valley floors (HAND $\approx$ 0) cannot rise; ridges can.

::: {.callout-note}
## The discharge coefficient is not free
A ridge water table genuinely sits tens of metres above the valley stream — a normal and *persistent*
state, sustained by continuous recharge down a long, slow flow path. An arbitrary discharge coefficient
drains that baseline away at metres per month. The coefficient is therefore anchored so that the
**observed baseline water table is a steady state**: at the long-term mean recharge $R_{ref}$, discharge
exactly balances recharge, and storms perturb around that equilibrium.
:::

Streamflow — the quantity a gauge measures — is therefore
$Q_{\text{stream}} = \text{runoff} + \text{interflow} + \text{baseflow}$, and the water table
balances $S_y\,\mathrm{d}h = \text{recharge} - \text{baseflow}$.

## Snow: the recharge clock

Recharge in this domain is governed by **snowmelt, not rainfall**. Precipitation peaks in November; the
snowpack peaks in February; **melt release peaks in April — and so does the observed water table.** The
snowpack, not the unsaturated zone, is what lags the system. Melt delivers ~312 mm/yr (≈20% of
precipitation) in spring, when rainfall *and* ET are low, so an unusually large share of it becomes
recharge rather than quickflow or evaporation.

A degree-day partition holds precipitation as SWE until melt. Its parameters are calibrated against
**30 SNOTEL SWE stations** within the domain (881–1414 m), using each station's own precipitation and
temperature so that the rain/snow-line smearing of a 4 km gridded product does not enter the
calibration.

::: {.callout-important}
## Melt-out timing, not SWE RMSE
The calibration target is **when the snow leaves**, not the RMSE of the SWE series. RMSE is dominated by
the accumulation season, and minimising it selects a melt factor slow enough that the pack lingers into
June and melt release falls in May — a month later than the observed water-table peak. Fitting melt-out
timing recovers the April peak. *Fit the quantity the physics depends on.*
:::

Snow is also **elevation-dependent**, and a domain mean destroys it: the mean of a cold mountain and a
warm lowland is ~7 °C and forms no snow at all. The forcing must be spatially distributed — over this
domain a January field spans −16.8 to +12.7 °C, with 23% of cells below freezing.

## Calibration and validation

The budget is constrained by **two independent classes of observation**, and the distinction is
essential:

- **Gauges constrain the fluxes.** Streamflow *is* the catchment water budget integrated over a basin,
  and a Lyne–Hollick baseflow separation splits it into quickflow (surface runoff + interflow) and
  baseflow (groundwater discharge). This is the only direct observation of the flux partition.
- **Wells constrain the state.** Depth to water, its seasonal amplitude, and its phase.

Wells alone are insufficient: the water table can be badly wrong while every field looks plausible,
because nothing in well data sees the flux partition directly. River discharge does.

Each parameter is pinned by a **different** observation, so the fit is constrained rather than tuned:

| parameter | constrained by |
|---|---|
| $S_y$ (specific yield) | water-table seasonal **amplitude** |
| $K_a$ (anisotropy) | **runoff coefficient** $Q/P$ |
| $R_{ref}$ (river-sink strength) | **baseflow index** |

Calibration is performed at the **daily** step — the step at which the model forecasts — because the
daily and monthly formulations drain differently and a monthly fit does not transfer.

| | model | observed |
|---|---|---|
| **baseflow index** | **0.52** | 0.47 (Lyne–Hollick separation, 6 gauges) |
| **runoff coefficient $Q/P$** | **0.71** | 0.57–0.73 (per-basin closure) |
| **seasonal amplitude** | **1.02 m** | 1.06 m (26,816 shallow-well observations) |
| water-table peak month | 5 | 4 |

![**The water budget closed against observations.** Left: the calibrated model against the two
independent observation classes — gauges constrain the fluxes, wells constrain the state. Centre: the
seasonal cycle, governed by the snowmelt clock — precipitation peaks in November, but melt and the water
table peak in April. Right: the flux partition.](assets/validation_domain.png){#fig-validation}

Precipitation is averaged over each gauge's **own watershed** (NLDI polygons), not over the analysis
domain: the gauged basins are Cascade headwater catchments receiving far more orographic precipitation
than a domain mean, and comparing basin discharge against domain-mean rainfall is not a closure.
Comparison against wells is likewise made **at the well locations** — wells are drilled where people
live and are not a random sample of the terrain.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Residuals, stated
The fitted $K_a = 2$ lies **below** the 10–100 literature range for forest-soil anisotropy. On steep
terrain a value of 20 drives $f_{lat}$ to ~0.82 — 82% of drainage shed laterally — which starves
recharge and drives $Q/P$ to 0.96. Either the literature range does not transfer to a 90 m cell, or this
single term absorbs other processes.

The water table peaks one month **late**. The direction is informative: an unsaturated travel-time lag
would make it *later still*, so that mechanism cannot explain the residual. Spin-up and the river-sink
timescale are the likelier causes.
:::

## The soil-moisture bucket

The temporal signal is a monthly Thornthwaite–Mather [-@thornthwaite1957water] balance in a single
root-zone bucket of capacity $\mathrm{AWC}=(\theta_{fc}-\theta_{wp})\,z_r$, forced by **precipitation**
$P$ and reference ET (the source is swappable — PRISM, a reanalysis, or a forecast, see
[Forecast](06-forecast.qmd)):

$$
S_t = \begin{cases}
\min\!\big(S_{t-1} + (P_t - \mathrm{PET}_t),\; \mathrm{AWC}\big), & P_t \ge \mathrm{PET}_t,\\[4pt]
\mathrm{AWC}\,\exp\!\big(-\mathrm{APWL}_t / \mathrm{AWC}\big), & P_t < \mathrm{PET}_t,
\end{cases}
$$

with APWL the accumulated potential water loss. The relative wetness $w_t=S_t/\mathrm{AWC}\in[0,1]$
fills the fine 90 m static envelope, $\theta_t=\theta_{wp}+w_t(\theta_{fc}-\theta_{wp})$, capped at
$\theta_{sat}$ — so the 90 m spatial texture is the static envelope while the temporal signal stays at
the driver scale.

## The groundwater-level pipeline

The operational GWL estimate writes the field in **anomaly space**, so each stage owns one term:

$$
h(x,t)=\underbrace{\overline{\mathrm{DTW}}(x)}_{\text{baseline mean}}+\underbrace{\Delta h(x,t)}_{\text{dynamics}}+\underbrace{r(x,t)}_{\text{assimilation residual}}.
$$

The **baseline** is an observation-anchored random forest on **HAND** (Height Above Nearest Drainage)
and SOLUS/terrain covariates — deliberately excluding coordinates so it cannot memorise well locations.
The **dynamics** sit on a ladder of increasing physical content: the lowest rung is a static-gain
climate-response β-map (per-site regression on SPI-3, ΔSWE, PDO), which reproduces *correlation* but no
memory; the higher rungs add a transfer-function/linear-reservoir memory (the same water table advanced
above). The **residual** is ordinary kriging of the observed well anomalies, precision-weighted into the
state ([Assimilation](04-assimilation.qmd)).
